message.content: Inter-Process Communication (IPC) is a technique where adversaries exploit IPC mechanisms to execute arbitrary code or commands locally. IPC is essential for processes to share data, communicate, and synchronize execution, and is used to prevent deadlocks. Adversaries can abuse IPC mechanisms, which vary by operating system, to achieve their goals. For instance, Windows uses Dynamic Data Exchange (DDE) and Component Object Model (COM), while Linux employs sockets and pipes. Higher-level execution mediums, such as Command and Scripting Interpreters, may also utilize IPC. Additionally, remote services like Distributed Component Object Model (DCOM) can facilitate remote IPC execution. Notable malware such as Cyclops Blink and HyperStack have demonstrated the ability to exploit IPC for malicious purposes. Mitigation strategies include monitoring and restricting IPC mechanisms, employing strict access controls, and using endpoint detection and response (EDR) tools to detect and block suspicious IPC activities.
